query: magnesium oxide effect on alginate mechanical properties

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Composition
retrieved_docs.content: Alginate is an irreversible hydrocolloid material that can reproduce soft and hard tissue details when in the presence of water. Alginates are salts produced from the combination of alginic acid with either sodium, calcium, potassium, or magnesium. [3] Alginic acid is a polysaccharide extracted from brown algae, a member of the Phaeophyceae family found primarily in America. [3] Dental alginates are available as a powder designed to be mixed with water. The powder contains sodium or potassium alginates, filler particles, calcium sulfate (reactor), fluoride (accelerator), and sodium phosphate (retarder). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Setting Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The setting mechanism of alginate is a chemical reaction between the salt sodium alginate and the reactor calcium sulfate. [3] This biphasic chemical reaction has a slowing phase and a setting phase. [3] When the powder is mixed with water, the calcium sulfate reactor initially combines with the sodium phosphate retarder, allowing for adequate working time. [3] After all the retarder is consumed, the remaining calcium sulfate reacts with the alginic salt to form insoluble calcium alginate in gel form. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Technique or Treatment -- Mixing
retrieved_docs.content: Semiautomatic mechanical mixing devices, available since 1978, create a less viscous mixture with fewer bubbles. [8] Mechanical mixing may also improve the compressive strength and elastic recovery of alginates. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Technique or Treatment -- Mixing
retrieved_docs.content: The mixing method can significantly affect the elastic recovery and tensile strength of the impression. Dental alginates are often manually mixed, but proprietary mechanical mixing devices are available. Alginate is supplied as a powder with a measuring scoop and a cylindrical cup for measuring water. The recommended powder-to-water ratio is usually 1:1 but is set by the manufacturer.

retrieved_docs.title: Alginate -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Alginate is a hydrocolloid widely used as an impression material in dentistry. It is derived from alginic acid, present in brown algae. It is an irreversible and elastic impression material. [13] See Image. Alginate, Impression Making. 1. Composition: Sodium or potassium alginate (15%) - dissolves in water and forms a viscous sol Calcium sulfate (16%) - reactor Diatomaceous earth (60%) - filler Zinc oxide (4%) - filler Potassium titanium fluoride (3%) - accelerator Sodium phosphate (2%) - retarder Glycol (in dust-free alginates) Pigments and flavoring agents 2. Indications: Diagnostic casts for treatment planning Orthodontic study models Impressions to fabricate partial and complete dentures, and temporomandibular disorder appliances For duplicating models 3. Instruments and armamentarium: flexible rubber bowl, curved spatula, measuring scoop, perforated impression tray, alginate powder, and water.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: 5. Setting reaction: It is a sol-to-gel transformation divided into 2 steps: retardation reaction and gelation reaction. First, calcium sulfate reacts with trisodium phosphate to form calcium sulfate and sodium sulfate. The second stage begins after all the trisodium phosphate has been consumed, providing the optimum working time for manipulating alginate. In the gelation reaction, potassium alginate reacts with calcium sulfate to form calcium alginate gel. 6. Advantages: Affordable Short manipulation time Simple technique Few armamentaria required Good impression (even in the presence of undercuts), all in a single step [16] [17] 7. Disadvantages: Low tear strength Dimensional instability 8. Modified alginates:

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Setting Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The retarder can slow the setting reaction, giving rise to dental alginates with different working times. Type I is a fast set that hardens within 1 to 2 minutes, and type II is a standard set that hardens within 2 to 5 minutes. [3] The setting reaction time of dental alginates also depends on the water temperature used for the mixture. Warmer water speeds up the setting reaction, and colder water slows the reaction down. [3] The water-to-powder ratio also affects the setting reaction; the hardening reaction is faster with more powder. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Technique or Treatment -- Loading
retrieved_docs.content: Alginic adhesives are required to dry for five minutes and must be applied before preparing the alginate. [3] Rinsing the mouth with a mixture of mouthwash and water before the impression is taken removes mucin and reduces the surface tension, avoiding air bubbles. [7] The freshly mixed alginate is loaded into the previously selected tray. Any excess material should be removed using the spatula. Some remaining fresh material can be placed on the occlusal surfaces of the teeth to be impressed to improve the reproduction of occlusal anatomy. A small amount of alginate can also be put on the palate.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Dustless alginates: manufactured to prevent inhalation of alginate particles, which may cause respiratory problems. The alginate particles are coated with glycol or glycerine to make them denser. Chromatic alginates: change colors during manipulation to guide mixing time and setting time. In the form of sol or paste (containing water) and a reactor (plaster of Paris) separately. Agar Agar is another hydrophilic colloid impression material like alginate, but it is reversible. It is the first elastic impression material to be used in dentistry. 1. Composition: Chemically, agar is a sulfuric ester of the galactan complex, and agar is available as a tray or syringe material. Agar (gelling agent) Borax (strengthener) Potassium sulfate (gypsum hardener) Alkyl benzoate (preservatives) Water (reaction medium > 80%) Fillers, flavoring, and coloring agents

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Properties
retrieved_docs.content: However, several downsides of dental alginates have been described. Alginates provide a less accurate reproduction than elastomers; they have low tear strength and are likely to tear when removed from deep undercuts, such as interproximal and subgingival areas; they can only be poured once, producing only one plaster model; and they have poor dimensional stability when pouring is delayed. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Alginate -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The building blocks of alginates are guluronic and mannuronic acid, both monosaccharides closely related to glucose. These building blocks form long chains that, with the addition of water, generate an anionic polysaccharide scaffold within an aqueous matrix. Varying the proportions of guluronic and mannuronic acid generates alginate compounds with differing physical and chemical properties. [2] With the addition of an ionic crosslinker such as calcium or barium, alginates form a resilient, flexible, solid/semisolid hydrogel. These hydrogels have been used extensively as a tissue interface in wound dressings and as a construct for encapsulating cells and other therapeutic compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: Alginate -- Issues of Concern -- Variability
retrieved_docs.content: Alginates crosslink to form hydrogels under a generally flexible set of conditions, which allows for significant variability in the composition and characteristics of the resultant biomaterial. A wide variety of compounds can be combined with alginates without disrupting their ability to crosslink, including therapeutic molecules, extracellular matrix components, and other biomaterials like chitosan. Various crosslinking agents and methods can be employed to generate hydrogels with a range of stiffness and degradative characteristics. This compositional flexibility allows alginate to act as a versatile, modifiable, biologically compatible biomaterial construct. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Very few allergic reactions to alginates have been reported. Alginate impression material is contraindicated in patients with a severe allergy to crystalline silica, calcium sulfate, or potassium titanium fluoride.

retrieved_docs.title: Alginate -- Clinical Significance -- Alginates in Wound Dressings
retrieved_docs.content: Alginate hydrogels have extensive utilization as wound interfaces in dressings intended to support healing following burns, pressure ulcers, venous ulcers, and other exudative cutaneous wounds. While the indications of alginate wound dressings are different in each of these contexts, a few underlying principles are generally relevant. Because alginates allow for the relatively free diffusion of water and solutes, alginate dressings are thought to generate a relatively physiologic environment at the wound interface that encourages healing. Additionally, some research postulates that calcium release from alginate hydrogels crosslinked with calcium stimulates coagulative pathways and which results in a healing response. While alginate dressings see extensive use in wound healing, few well-controlled studies definitively link their usage to improvements in outcomes. [8] It remains undetermined whether this is due to a lack of published evidence or a lack of benefit achieved with these dressings.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Impression materials are classified as either elastic or non-elastic based on mechanical properties. [1] Elastic materials are capable of stretching, compressing, and recovering after deformation. Elastic materials comprise reversible and irreversible hydrocolloids, addition and condensation silicones, polysulfides, and polyether. [2] Nonelastic or rigid impression materials include impression compounds, zinc oxide eugenol, and impression waxes. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is a medical formulation that incorporates several specific components, including kalcium‑glycerolfosfát, dibasic potassium phosphate, and sodium hyaluronate. Kalcium‑glycerolfosfát contributes calcium and phosphate ions that can support mineral balance, while dibasic potassium phosphate provides additional phosphate and potassium ions to help maintain physiological pH and electrolyte homeostasis. Sodium hyaluronate, a high‑molecular‑weight glycosaminoglycan, offers viscoelastic properties that are valuable for joint lubrication and tissue repair. Together, these ingredients define the composition of the replacement preparation, highlighting its role in delivering essential minerals and lubricating agents for therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium citrate is an osmotic laxative that may be used to treat إِمْساك, but its administration is contraindicated in patients with ثقب معوي because it can worsen a perforated intestine. إِمْساك is also clinically associated with تَقَيُّء, indicating that constipation and diarrhea often occur together in functional bowel disorders. When considering magnesium citrate for symptom relief, clinicians must ensure the absence of ثقب معوي and be aware of the potential shift from إِمْساك to تَقَيُّء, tailoring treatment to maintain safe and effective gastrointestinal management.

All magnesium‑based agents (magnesium sulfate, magnesium citrate, magnesium oxide, magnesium phosphate) share a major contraindication in renal failure (قصور الكلى); magnesium citrate is additionally contraindicated in intestinal perforation (ثقب معوي). Calcium pantothenate exerts its biological activity through enzyme‑interaction mechanisms and phosphate‑chelating activity, contributing to metabolic pathways, cellular activity alteration and bone health. Clinical guidance notes a contraindication in patients with kidney stones, while its phosphate‑chelating property is suggested to help prevent osteoporosis. Vitamin D (cholecalciferol) is a key component of multivitamin tablets, liquid iron/multivitamin preparations and prenatal formulas, often combined with calcium sources such as calcium oyster shell. Its primary pharmacologic action is inhibition of bone resorption, supporting skeletal density and reducing the risk of osteoporotic changes. Understanding that bismuth subnitrate can help alleviate diarrheal symptoms while magnesium hydroxide may precipitate them is important for clinicians when selecting appropriate treatments and managing potential side effects in patients with gastrointestinal disturbances.

It is incorporated into cardioplegic solutions (Del Nido, Hyposol), the Tis‑U‑Sol irrigation solution, and various magnesium‑sulfate‑based electrolyte mixtures. The umbrella entity IRON/MULTIVITAMINS groups a large family of multivitamin‑mineral products that integrate elemental iron (in forms including ferrous fumarate, ferrous asparto‑glycinate, iron sucrose, polysaccharide iron complex, ferric pyrophosphate) with extensive vitamin complexes (vitamins A, B‑complex – B12, thiamine, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine, cyanocobalamin – C, D (cholecalciferol), E (α‑tocopherol acetate), K‑related compounds) and essential minerals (calcium carbonate, calcium ascorbate, calcium threonate, magnesium oxide, magnesium L‑threonate, zinc oxide, copper oxide, iodine, trace elements). Folic acid is a recurrent component of these formulations and is explicitly linked to the treatment of macrocytic anemia. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 142
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium salts—including magnesium orotate, magnesium oxide (oxid hořečnatý), magnesium sulfate (síran hořečnatý), magnesium aspartate (aspartát hořečnatý), magnesium citrate, magnesium gluconate, and magnesium pidolate—are used both as active agents and excipients in a variety of pharmaceutical formulations. Magnesium sulfate is the parent compound of calcium‑ and magnesium‑containing products and is present in Del Nido cardioplegia solution, Hyposol, an irrigation solution containing 0.2 mg/mL magnesium sulfate together with monobasic potassium phosphate, potassium chloride, sodium chloride, and sodium phosphate dihydrate, and a preparation described as magnesium sulfate/potassium chloride/sodium sulf. Osmotically acting laxatives are classified under the magnesium sulfate group and also include magnesium citrate; both magnesium sulfate and magnesium citrate are employed to treat constipation (إِمْساك) but are contraindicated in patients with renal failure (قصور الكلى), and magnesium citrate carries an additional risk of intestinal perforation (ثقب معوي). Suby’s G solution contains magnesium oxide, which also appears as an inactive ingredient in nicotine 2 mg chewing gum and is listed among osmotic laxatives together with lactulose. Magnesium aspartate and magnesium gluconate share the same renal contraindication. Magnesium pidolate is used as an inactive component in a camphor‑0.03 mg/mg/menthol‑0.03 mg/mg topical gel. Collectively, these relationships highlight the therapeutic role of various magnesium salts as laxatives, their incorporation into diverse drug products, and the critical safety consideration of avoiding magnesium‑containing preparations in individuals with compromised renal function or at risk for intestinal injury.
